mnemonics.title: Daemon Creation Steps
mnemonics.content: The 5 steps of daemon creation can be remembered as 'F S C C E': Fork, Setsid, Chdir, Close file descriptors, Exit parent and restart in child.

mnemonics.title: Common Daemons
mnemonics.content: Remember important daemons with phrase: 'I Cron At Syslog Sshd Httpd' - Init, Cron, Atd, Syslogd, SSHd, HTTPD.

acronyms.usage: Unique identifier for each running process, used to manage and send signals to daemons
First process in Unix/Linux, parent of all processes and orphan adoption
Super daemon that listens for network connections and starts other daemons on demand
Improved version of inetd with better security and access control
Daemon that runs scheduled commands periodically (from Greek 'chronos' meaning time)
Centralized system logging daemon that collects and stores system messages

visualTips.visualization: Draw a timeline: Boot → init starts → Daemon fork() → setsid() → loops waiting for events → terminates on signal/shutdown
Draw tree: init (PID 1) at top → children (daemons) below → grandchildren (processes started by daemons)
Box diagram: Parent → fork1 → Child1 → fork2 → Child2 (actual daemon) → Child1 exits → Child2 orphaned → init adopts Child2
Show stdin/stdout/stderr as three arrows pointing away from daemon process to /dev/null

analogies.analogy: Like a night security guard who works continuously in the background, watching for incidents and responding when needed, without being part of the day's regular work.
A daemon is like a 24-hour automated teller machine (always on, no user session needed), while a regular process is like a desktop application (requires active user session).
Like children who lose their parents being taken to an orphanage (init), where they're cared for (wait() called) until they can exist independently.
Inetd acts like a hotel receptionist - sits at the front desk, waits for visitors (connection requests), and then calls the appropriate service staff (daemon) to handle the visitor.
